grantees.name: New Jersey Council for the Humanities and Journalism + Design

grants.grantee: New Jersey Council for the Humanities and Journalism + Design
grants.purpose: to fund the development of a statewide journalism training program in partnership with six New Jersey community colleges that equips citizens with skills to tell the stories of their communities. Journalism + Design and NJCH piloted a similar program in South Jersey in spring 2022.

to support a new Report for America corps member to expand health coverage by focusing on mental health in underserved communities, at the intersection of the COVID-19 pandemic, health inequity, and rural New Jersey. The 2022 grant was funded in part by support from New Jersey Health Initiatives (NJHI), a statewide grantmaking program of the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation.
to hire a Washington, D.C.-based correspondent to provide regular reporting to NJ Spotlight News and a collaborative network of newsrooms across the state on developments affecting New Jersey.

to launch a community-reporter corps pilot program that trains residents in news gathering, writing, and production and provides paid reporting assignments that prioritize the information needs of underserved populations in the surrounding community, inspire civic participation, and build narrative power.
to support the placement of a student intern through the Statewide Paid Internship Program.

The Jersey Bee is a public service news and information provider that collaborates with the communities it serves to produce content that reflects local needs, encourages civic participation, and enhances the quality of life for BIPOC and working-class residents. The organization focuses on delivering accessible, relevant reporting that informs and empowers through newsletters, a community website, a local events calendar, social media, and SMS updates

grantees.name: Trenton Journal
focusAreas: Blue Engine Tech & Sustainability Accelerator Grant

grants.purpose: to train local contributors to increase the frequency of Trenton Journal’s editorial output, help change the narrative, bridge information gaps, and connect communities in Trenton. The Trenton Journal focuses on addressing the disparities faced by the Black and Brown community in Trenton using solutions-based journalism via a newsletter, video, and audio stories.

grants.purpose: Trenton Journal is a multimedia news outlet committed to filling information gaps and amplifying positive voices within the community through original, fact-based reporting. Over the past five years, the publication has served as a trusted source of local news, highlighting stories often overlooked by larger media and fostering civic engagement among Trenton residents.

grantees.name: Front Runner New Jersey
focusAreas: Blue Engine Tech & Sustainability Accelerator Grant

grants.purpose: to support Front Runner New Jersey, a new site led by a veteran journalist who seeks to maintain and expand its coverage and bring a new perspective on the people, events, and issues making an impact on the African American and Latino communities in Atlantic City and South Jersey. Front Runner brings a restorative perspective to news and information reporting.

Front Runner New Jersey.com is a news and information website dedicated to covering Black, Latino, and other marginalized communities across South Jersey. The platform serves two primary purposes: to amplify the voices of Black and Brown communities in the region and to educate the broader public about the people, events, and issues that matter to them. It accomplishes this by publishing original stories and actively engaging with communities to highlight what is most important to them.

grantees.name: The Jersey Vindicator
focusAreas: Blue Engine Tech & Sustainability Accelerator Grant

grants.purpose: to build The Jersey Vindicator — the “ProPublica of New Jersey” — with the goal of engaging and empowering the public through investigative journalism at the local, county, and state level in its publication, ultimately ensuring that residents — particularly those in underserved communities — are better informed about government, business, education, transportation, criminal justice reform, and the environment.

Blue Engine Technology & Sustainability Accelerator cohort members all received a $10,000 technology grant used to implement new technological solutions for the respective organizations. This includes, but is not limited to, software and hardware updates including website development, subscriptions for cloud-based applications, computer equipment, etc.

grants.purpose: to support the placement of a student intern through the Statewide Paid Internship Program.

The Jersey Vindicator onboarded journalists Steve Janoski and Jeff Pillets as staff reporter and contributing editor, achieving a more consistent publishing schedule and expanded accountability coverage. In addition, the newsroom contracted award-winning visual journalist and Spanish speaker Andres Kudacki as multimedia and engagement director. Kudacki is broadening statewide reach by expanding Spanish-language coverage and enhancing visual storytelling and social media presence. The Vindicator also established a physical office in Trenton, creating a visible and accessible hub for reporting.

grantees.name: Public Square Amplified
focusAreas: Blue Engine Tech & Sustainability Accelerator Grant

grants.purpose: to fund a program to recruit, train, and promote community reporters and journalism students and provide an outlet for their work in Caribbean, African, Indigenous, and Immigrant communities.

grants.category: Blue Engine Tech & Sustainability Accelerator Grant

grants.purpose:
PSA earned the National Headliner Award and an SPJ-NJ Award for its photo essay on Black childbirth. The 2025 Citizen as Journalist program was held for the third consecutive summer and received more than 50 inquiries and applications for its three-month curriculum. The newsroom has maintained a consistent publication schedule, and the addition of a freelance reporter has strengthened its capacity to cover breaking news within its editorial framework.
Since the launch of the Citizen Journalism class, 50 community reporters have been trained, contributing 195 stories that reflect the voices and experiences of their communities.

to support the Radio Jornalera NJ Communication Project, which brings together the development, production and dissemination of public service announcements from the Jornalero and Domestic Laborer Communicators throughout the state. The Public Service Announcements are on topics that affect daily life, such as housing rights, workers’ rights, wage theft, health and safety at work, healthy lifestyle, and/or climate change.

Radio Jornalera NJ has expanded its reach and capacity this year, now streaming 12 regular shows on Facebook and Spotify. The team of street reporters has completed two rounds of training and is actively producing original content. The newsroom has trained a rapid response team in Mercer County, with audience engagement ranging from 42,000 to 97,000 views on social media platforms. Radio Jornalera NJ is also participating in the GNI State News Sustainability Accelerator program hosted by Blue Engine, gaining tools and strategies to strengthen long-term sustainability. In addition, a Substack channel has been launched to provide written news in both English and Spanish. The studio’s technology and design have been upgraded with new cameras and an improved layout to create a more comfortable environment for live, in-person recordings.

grantees.name: Slice of Culture @ Saint Peter's University

grants.grantee: Slice of Culture @ Saint Peter's University
grants.purpose: to support Slice of Culture, a group of young BIPOC storytellers who are bringing civic engagement to Jersey City and Hudson County through a series of how-to projects and informational stories targeted to immigrant families and communities of color. It is opening doors to aspiring journalists who are committed to strong, local journalism and stories that matter to the community.

to support the launch of Black in Jersey as a reliable news and information hub serving New Jersey’s Black communities, and also to provide support for the organization’s first statehouse and local government reporting cohort, and to launch its incubator program featuring Millennial and Generation Z-led news services — including the Hotspot Podcast.

Black In Jersey’s most notable improvements came through staffing additions. Frank Santos stepped into the leadership role of Head of Creative Marketing. Adrienne Bauldock was brought on to refine the organization’s social media presence and community engagement strategy while taking on a larger role in event coordination.
The publication’s most engaging story focused on Camden and was co-produced by SJIEP fellow Jewel Justice and team member Frank Santos. It covered the environmental hazard caused by the European Metal Recycling Company (EMR), which caught fire and forced thousands of residents to evacuate. Black In Jersey’s team was among the first publications to break the story and was one of the few, if not the only, outlets present at the subsequent hearings and community organizing events around the city’s incinerator. The piece went viral, drawing over 15,000 page views and sparking local action through the Fight Fire with Fire advocacy group formed by local Camden residents.

grantees.name: Atlantic City Focus
focusAreas: Blue Engine Tech & Sustainability Accelerator Grant

grants.purpose: to fund a project to create a Civic Engagement Guide for Atlantic City that provides residents with a comprehensive directory of information on local elections, community resources, and leadership.

grants.purpose: to launch its Spanish publication, “Noticias”, making its news coverage more representative of Hammonton’s southern New Jersey community. The paper offers news articles in Spanish and initiates outreach to seasonal agricultural workers and more permanent members of the local Spanish-speaking community. It also funds freelance reporters to cover municipal meetings in news deserts in Atlantic County and Camden County. Noticias was the first local newspaper to offer news articles in Spanish (Mexican Dialect).

to fund the expansion of its coverage to neighboring communities in South-Central New Jersey that currently lack local news. It will fund additional staff, including a new position and an expanded freelance budget involving Rowan students, enabling the Gazette to provide weekly coverage of local events, activities, and sports. The project also offers young journalists practical experience, supporting the Gazette's mission to deliver reliable news in an area facing a decline in traditional news sources.
to support the placement of a student intern through the Statewide Paid Internship Program.

grantees.name: Ridge View Echo
focusAreas: Blue Engine Tech & Sustainability Accelerator Grant

grants.purpose: to address the loss of local-news outlets by launching the Greater Blairstown News Project online publication, the Ridge View Echo, reporting on Blairstown, Frelinghuysen, Hardwick and Knowlton, all Warren County communities with no other dedicated local news sources. This effort provides residents with vital and timely government announcements about public health and safety, and foster greater connection to and awareness of local issues across northwest New Jersey.

Blue Engine Technology & Sustainability Accelerator cohort members all received a $10,000 technology grant used to implement new technological solutions for the respective organizations. grants.purpose: to fund a fellowship opportunity that will cultivate college students as future journalists in New Jersey and train them to report, write, and engage with local communities by covering the South Asian American community.

This fellowship opportunity will cultivate college students as future journalists in New Jersey and train them to report, write and engage with local communities by covering the South Asian American community. It will also result in coverage of this underreported community and drive engagement through community events and social media.

to fund journalism training for neighborhood residents to learn how to use storytelling as an advocacy strategy. This includes oral histories, podcasts, video reports and documentaries. The funding also is being used to establish a new media training, production, and webcast center through Clinton Hill’s Citizen Journalism Program.

The newsroom is developing a regular publishing schedule and employs trained community members to report on local issues, share resident perspectives, and strengthen civic awareness across the neighborhood. To advance this effort, CHCA engaged Sheryl Hilliard Tucker as a planning consultant to shape the newsroom’s vision and guide content development, and hired Jordann Peters, a recent journalism graduate, to assist with news production.

to foster civic engagement in Newark public schools through: in-depth reporting on school segregation litigation and federal Covid relief spending; adding a state education policy reporting fellow; a texting service to inform the community about school board actions; a 2024 school board election guide; and first person student essays published through Chalkbeat Newark’s Student Voices program.
to train Camden high school students in interviewing and media reporting so they can become community reporters and storytellers. The grant also will fund voter education programs and training.
to fund a Journalism Mentorship Program that provides high school students with early awareness and readiness training for undergraduate collegiate journalism programs.

grantees.name: Center for Cooperative Media: Research and Development

grants.grantee: Center for Cooperative Media: Research and Development
grants.purpose: to fund a researcher at the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University to conduct research on the New Jersey local news ecosystem. The researcher will also conduct learning and impact evaluation for the Consortium.
to fund the “Community-driven Journalism Project” to develop journalists, focusing on Black and Brown workers in low wage jobs across New Jersey.

to develop and implement each year a 15-week training and internship program for youth/citizens in foster care and for children of incarcerated people to tell the stories of their peers for Hopeloft’s local news site, Follow South Jersey. This grant was funded in part in the first year by support from New Jersey Health Initiatives (NJHI), a statewide grantmaking program of the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation.

grants.purpose: to fund New Jersey Urban News, an online news platform dedicated to covering New Jersey’s vibrant African American community through informative stories and thorough coverage. NJUN will use this funding to hire a full-time editor and reporter, improving the depth and quality of local reporting for New Jersey's urban communities. This initiative aims to address critical issues, amplify marginalized voices, and foster civic engagement by ensuring residents are well-informed and connected to local resources.
to support the placement of a student intern through the Statewide Paid Internship Program.

grantees.name: New Jersey State House News Service

grants.grantee: New Jersey State House News Service
grants.purpose: to fund The News Service, a nonprofit, collaborative effort that trains and develops student journalists by providing professional reporting on government and politics from Trenton’s statehouse for use by independent local news outlets across New Jersey. The program offers college level students hands-on experience, with guidance from an experienced, full-time editor.

to train residents, local reporters, and mediamakers to respond to Newark residents’ information needs and concerns that capture the true essence of the city. Its lab comprises resident-reporters that produce unique coverage of issues impacting diverse communities in Newark that lack traditional media coverage and are not covered by traditional media.

Prior to the September board meeting, we received an emergency request to support the New Jersey Monitor in its effort to hire healthcare reporter Lilo Stainton, who was laid off from NJ Spotlight following federal funding losses. We requested your support, which the Grants Committee approved for presentation to the full board.
After further discussion with New Jersey Monitor editor Terrence McDonald, it was determined that funds for the planned hire would not be needed until January 2026. To allow time to finalize details with the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation (RWJF) contribution of $150K, we agreed to wait until the December grantmaking cycle to award this project - provided all conditions are met.
Since then, RWJF has awarded the Consortium $150,000 over two years to support this position. The Consortium will grant these funds to the New Jersey Monitor over a three-year period. We are now requesting the board’s consideration to supplement this support with an additional $60,000 over three years.

to work with Rutgers University to establish Radio Cosecha, a twice-weekly Spanish-language internet-radio program for the state’s Spanish-speaking immigrant community, telling the stories of seasonal agricultural workers in South Jersey. Cosecha researches and delivers important local, national, and international news in an accessible format, in order to achieve greater civic participation within New Jersey’s immigrant community.

grantees.name: Center for Cooperative Media: NJ News Commons Spanish Translation Service

grants.grantee: Center for Cooperative Media: NJ News Commons Spanish Translation Service
grants.purpose: to launch the NJ News Commons Spanish Translation Service, partnering with the largest Spanish-language and mainstream media outlets in the state to translate statewide news — especially elections-related content and voter information — into Spanish. This project builds on previous pilot work around translating pandemic coverage.

grantees.name: Corporation for New Jersey Local Media

grants.grantee: Corporation for New Jersey Local Media
grants.purpose: to fund the continuation of the work of the Northern NJ News Collaborative on its “Stormwater Matters” initiative and will grow community engagement.

grants.purpose: to fund a program in collaboration with New Brunswick Tomorrow to create a community-based memory and story-telling project, Teatro Esperanza, in the Esperanza and Unity Square neighborhoods of New Brunswick.

grants.category: Blue Engine Tech & Sustainability Accelerator Grant

grants.purpose: to support The Central New Jersey Network, a non-profit community TV station based in Somerset County. Funding will be used to expand its programming and internship opportunities, with a focus on amplifying diverse voices and strengthening community connections. The project will cover local events in underrepresented areas like Trenton and rural Central Jersey while recruiting interns from local colleges, particularly from underrepresented and impoverished communities, to promote diversity in media production.

to support an ongoing statewide storytelling project focused on documenting resident perspectives about COVID-19 vaccination. Insights have been incorporated into a digital and print “feature” for local dissemination with resident experiences, recommendations, and best practices for communicating about the vaccine in a community-centered, culturally relevant way that enhances access to documented stories, promotes information-sharing, and strengthens communities.

to create Newark Community Voices, a digital-media and community-organizing training program for Newark youth. The program produces and distributes multimedia health and environment stories that center Newark community-information needs, with a focus on an oral history of the advocates who worked to clean up Newark’s drinking water.

grantees.name: Center for Cooperative Media: South Jersey Information Equity Project

grants.grantee: Center for Cooperative Media: South Jersey Information Equity Project
grants.purpose: to support the expansion of the South Jersey Information Equity Project (SJEIP) from a cohort of 5 to 10 fellows annually and establish an SJIEP community reporting workshop series to further engage local storytellers through intergenerational learning and co-creation workshops.

to fund stipends for student interns providing high quality news coverage of climate change in South Jersey, including the impact on the environment, agriculture, the Pine Barrens, beaches, tourism, and wetlands.

to create an incubator for emerging community reporters and local media makers. The work the community reporters produce over the course of the training sessions — including news articles, news broadcasts for radio, news videos and documentary short subjects, podcasts, and social media posts — is published and disseminated through a website and a network of local media partners. The project has focused on live streaming city council and board of education meetings in Asbury Park.
to support the placement of a student intern through the Statewide Paid Internship Program.
To support expanded bilingual coverage in Hudson County through new reporting staff, print editions, and community engagement events.

to support the Garden State Initiative (GSI) in its efforts to leverage expert resources to develop a comprehensive data warehouse and user-friendly reporting system that provides timely analysis of past and current state budgets, as well as the proposed FY 2026 budget, with a particular focus on so-called budget resolutions (more commonly referred to as earmarks). Guided by budget experts and financial journalists, this initiative aims to enhance transparency by making otherwise opaque public expenditures more accessible and understandable, ensuring that critical fiscal information serves the broader public policy interest. For the current fiscal year, over $1 billion were allocated to these resolutions.
To support the launch of a multimedia civic news platform created by and for Muslim-Americans, including staff hiring, student internships, and community engagement.

This calendar year, Ben Hulac has produced 135 stories and made numerous appearances on NJ Spotlight News with Briana Vannozzi. On social media, he is a frequent and engaging presence on the NJ Spotlight News branded account, regularly breaking down his stories and the issues behind them while maintaining steady audience interaction. He also contributes to the monthly NJSN Coffee Chat virtual events for members and the weekly Statehouse podcast Hey NJ.

A major leadership transition was completed with the appointment of a new Executive Director and Managing Editor. Coverage of municipal, school board, and community issues expanded and reader engagement grew through a weekly newsletter, community submissions, and new partnerships with Sussex County Community College. RVE participated in the Blue Engine Collaborative Accelerator, which contributed to a 67% increase in newsletter growth and supported development of a donation-based revenue model.

to pilot an initiative to train and pay community members as reporters covering the local city council, planning board, board of education, and casino reinvestment authority meetings, and to develop new ways to engage community members in the decision-making processes that impact their lives. Traditional journalism methods are not meeting the larger Atlantic City community’s information needs.

to support efforts to expand the reach and enhance the impact of Garden State Leaders, a statewide leadership and advocacy training program for New Jersey residents with lived experience of homelessness and poverty. The funds will enable them to formalize their ongoing statewide support and outcome tracking, and to pilot a local leadership program in collaboration with the Mercer Alliance to End Homelessness, for replication in other counties.

grantees.name: Center for Cooperative Media: Statewide Voting Guide

grants.grantee: Center for Cooperative Media: Statewide Voting Guide
grants.purpose: to fund the making of the 2023 NJ Statewide Voting Guide, which helped encourage civic engagement. Citizens also had an interactive opportunity to vote their choices on statewide and local issues.

Conducted in-depth impact tracking for the Consortium’s grantees, holding monthly meetings with 35+ organizations to provide guidance on tracking outcomes and identifying meaningful impact metrics for their work.

Led the production and publication of the second impact report for the Consortium, a critical deliverable completed after it became clear that the Consortium’s state funding might not be renewed. Hanna contributed preliminary insights through an impact-at-a-glance one-pager, developed in collaboration with Madi McCool, allowing key findings to be presented ahead of state budget decisions. Ultimately, these efforts supported the partial renewal of the Consortium’s funding at $2.5 million.

to launch and develop “be.tv eStudios,” an initiative teaching Burlington County students and young adults, ages 14-24, storytelling and media production skills with an emphasis on conducting interviews with professionals, business leaders, educators, etc., and using online gaming techniques for capturing and promoting interview highlights. It also teaches media and business skills and has involved students in organizing and staging esports tournaments and programs.

to grow the content, readership, and capacity of a fledgling digital journalism program and school-wide digital news source by integrating curriculum from varied elective courses and leveraging the expertise of a local college or university.

grants.purpose: to support The Daily Targum, Rutgers University’s student-run news outlet, aims to expand its digital presence, attract advertisers, and secure a 2025 student fee referendum to restore University funding. As one of New Jersey's largest journalism training grounds, employing over 30 student journalists each year, the Targum aligns with the Consortium's mission by producing civic news and covering the Rutgers communities of New Brunswick, Newark, and Camden.

grants.purpose: to undertake a community-centered storytelling project in Camden documenting the challenges and opportunities of living a long, healthful life amidst environmental injustice by interviewing, facilitating discussions, and documenting the personal narratives of community members.

to support Inside Climate News in strengthening New Jersey’s local environmental reporting ecosystem through on-the-ground freelance journalism and collaborations with local media outlets, aiming to fill gaps in climate and environmental coverage while building a sustained reporting presence in communities across the state.

to launch a news agency that produces accessible, multimedia reports for and about people with disabilities in New Jersey. This grant was funded in part by support from New Jersey Health Initiatives (NJHI), a statewide grantmaking program of the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation.

to develop from an operating prototype a repository to collect, clean, host, integrate, analyze, and present data and resulting insights to local investigative journalists, community activists, small businesses, academicians, and students.

The translated stories now span a broad range of topics, including immigration, education, healthcare, housing, transportation, the economy, and the environment. Since January 2025, heightened federal enforcement actions under the Trump administration have driven an increase in immigration-related arrests at workplaces and other locations, and a significant portion of recent translations has focused on immigration as a result. Over the past three years, interest in republishing STNS translated stories has grown steadily, supported by project leadership’s efforts to standardize translation practices while preserving cultural nuance and linguistic accuracy. This growth in republication across both English- and Spanish-language outlets reflects a wider recognition among English-focused news partners of the importance of Spanish translation in reaching and informing diverse audiences. As of October 2025, approximately 190 stories have been translated into Spanish, and the number of STNS partners has expanded to 19, including seven Spanish-language and 12 English-language organizations.

Significant changes have taken place since the last grant period, most notably the end of Montclair State University’s formal relationship with the program following statewide rollbacks of DEI initiatives. As a result, Montclair no longer provides direction, staffing, or financial support. In response to this shift, Velvet McNeil has assumed the role of Executive Director, taking on expanded leadership responsibilities to guide the program forward. During this period, the organization also hosted a full-day professional development session led by award-winning investigative journalist Nicole Carr, formerly of ProPublica, focused on the legacy of the Black press and approaches to long-form investigative reporting.

to hold neighborhood listening sessions in the 2023-2024 growing season through its Jersey Roots Youth Program, in collaboration with its Resilient Roots Farm Project, and to learn about what local issues are top of mind for its Black, Brown, and Southeast Asian neighbors who live, work, and play in East Camden neighborhoods.

to enable this online Haitian community radio organization to develop its own website, app, and weekly public service announcements, relevant to the more than 23,000 members of the Haitian community in Essex County. The project aim is to focus on translating and disseminating reliable information on civics, health, relevant news, and education and to create a platform where community members can address concerns and share experiences.

The goal of this initiative is to add an education and training component to Village Green’s coverage by hiring and mentoring high school and college students. The program will intentionally recruit and support a diverse group of aspiring journalists while expanding both the topics and geographic scope of Village Green’s reporting.
to fund the creation of a podcasting studio and support the digital journalism program for students in Wayne Township.

grants.purpose: to support Let My People Learn-HistorVic Education, a project that helps young people learn more about the workings of local governments and how policies and laws impact their communities.
to create a comprehensive and searchable archive of local-government activity spanning more than two decades. The project was completed and the tool made available to area residents and journalists.

will add more features to its health information website to help residents statewide — including native Spanish speakers and others in underserved communities — get real-time information about vaccination appointment locations and vaccine availability.
to fund the creation of a new community centered news platform covering Trenton, in partnership with COMPAS, Pod City Trenton, Kean University and The College of New Jersey.

to manage the library-based Paterson Information Hub to provide the community with training in citizen journalism and news-production technology. The alliance also aimed to convene local events to assess community-information needs.
to support the Two River Times with costs associated with its organization’s transition from a for-profit to a non-profit.
to support the expansion of access to the Healthy NewsWorks literacy program and engage students and schools in Camden, New Jersey through community health journalism.
To support student-led investigative reporting, print distribution, and equipment upgrades that strengthen journalism training at TCNJ.

Chalkbeat’s newsroom in Essex County focused exclusively on schools, provides information to families, educators, and community leaders with a focus on those in historically underserved communities, so they can advocate effectively for better educational outcomes. 
The newsroom covers urgent issues in Newark’s schools by examining policy changes, legislative developments, and school operations, while also providing essential data and context. Through its in-depth reporting, Chalkbeat Newark helps expand public understanding of the complex challenges and innovations shaping education, ultimately supporting more informed and engaged communities.

Slice of Culture (SOC) is an online media platform dedicated to local news, civic engagement, and community empowerment. Led by a team of young BIPOC storytellers, many of whom were raised in and currently reside in Hudson County. SOC produces accessible, engaging content that covers community events, civic issues, and public resource guides.

grantees.name: If I Can, You Can Inc.

grants.grantee: If I Can, You Can Inc.
grants.purpose: to help revitalize the online neighborhood news franchise TAPInto.net in Salem County, with the goal of sharing news articles across New Jersey tow

to launch an investigative project on the census, focusing on the immigrant and non-English speaking communities in New Brunswick. New Brunswick Today proposed to examine the extent to which those communities may have been undercounted in the most recent census and any resulting implications. The project intended to analyze the past three census counts to examine the effects of gentrification in the city.

to fund a census of student journalists and journalism programs around the state, along with the development of a central database of scholastic journalism websites and publications. The grant will also fund student press identification cards.

to fund a media publication centering Muslim news, with coverage affecting the New Jersey Muslim community. New Jersey contains the highest percentage of Muslims to the state population in the United States. The money also is helping Muslim.co expand its coverage of the Palestinian community in New Jersey, particularly in the context of the conflict in Gaza.

to fund the creation of a Journalism Talent Pipeline for aspiring journalists  of color or from an underrepresented community. Nationally, journalists of color make up less than 17% of newsrooms and just 13% of newsroom leadership.
